content:
# Romans Chapter 13 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 13 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 13:1 — "Let every person be subject" vs. "Every soul is to be subject" vs. "Everyone must submit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Every soul [is to] be subject to the governing authorities." | "Soul" for ψυχή — literal; "governing authorities" for ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις |
| LOGOS | "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities." | "Person" for ψυχή — natural English; jussive "let" |
| PNEUMA | "Everyone must submit to governing authorities." | "Must submit" — stronger obligation; drops "every person/soul" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "every soul" is literal but archaic in English. LOGOS's "let every person" preserves the universal scope. PNEUMA's "must submit" adds a modal of obligation not explicitly in the Greek imperative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:1 — "instituted by God" vs. "appointed by God" vs. "placed there by God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] existing have been appointed by God" | "Appointed" for τεταγμέναι — passive perfect participle |
| LOGOS | "those that exist have been instituted by God" | "Instituted" for τεταγμέναι — more formal |
| PNEUMA | "those in positions of authority have been placed there by God" | "Placed there" — spatial/concrete; adds "in positions of authority" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "instituted." This captures the formal, ordaining sense of τάσσω (to arrange, appoint, order) better than PNEUMA's casual "placed there." EMET's "appointed" is also acceptable.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:2 — "will incur judgment" vs. "will receive judgment on themselves" vs. "will be punished"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] having opposed will receive judgment on themselves" | "Receive judgment on themselves" — literal reflexive |
| LOGOS | "those who resist will incur judgment" | "Incur judgment" — concise legal language |
| PNEUMA | "they will be punished" | "Punished" — simplifies; drops "judgment" entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Incur judgment" preserves the judicial language (κρίμα). PNEUMA's "punished" is too specific — κρίμα is "judgment/verdict," which may or may not result in punishment. EMET's rendering is accurate but wordy.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:4 — "God's servant" vs. "a servant of God" — and "the sword"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For it is a servant of God to you for the good... it does not bear the sword in vain. For it is a servant of God, an avenger for wrath to the [one] practicing the evil." | Uses "it" — neuter for the authority; double "servant of God" |
| LOGOS | "for he is God's servant for your good... he does not bear the sword in vain. For he is the servant of God, an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer." | Uses "he" — personifies the ruler; "carries out God's wrath" |
| PNEUMA | "The authorities are God's servants, sent for your good... they have the power to punish you. They are God's servants, sent for the very purpose of punishing those who do what is wrong." | Plural "they"; "power to punish" for "bear the sword"; "sent" added twice |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The singular "he" follows the Greek singular and personifies the ruler appropriately. PNEUMA's "power to punish" replaces the concrete "bear the sword" (τὴν μάχαιραν φορεῖ), losing the specific image of state coercive power. PNEUMA also adds "sent" (not in Greek) and replaces "avenger" (ἔκδικος) with a paraphrase.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Bear the sword" and "avenger" retained.

---

## 13:5 — "for the sake of conscience" vs. "to keep a clear conscience"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not only because of the wrath but also because of the conscience" | "Because of the conscience" — literal |
| LOGOS | "not only to avoid God's wrath but also for the sake of conscience" | "For the sake of conscience" — idiomatic |
| PNEUMA | "not only to avoid punishment, but also to keep a clear conscience" | "Punishment" for ὀργή; "keep a clear conscience" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA changes "wrath" (ὀργή) to "punishment" (narrowing the meaning) and "for the sake of conscience" to "keep a clear conscience" (adding the adjective "clear" and changing the preposition to a verb phrase).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:6 — "ministers of God" vs. "government workers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they are ministers of God, attending continually to this very [thing]" | "Ministers" for λειτουργοί — liturgical/cultic term |
| LOGOS | "the authorities are ministers of God, attending to this very thing" | Same term; "ministers" |
| PNEUMA | "For government workers need to be paid. They are serving God in what they do." | "Government workers" — secularized; restructures entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul deliberately uses λειτουργοί ("ministers" — a word with cultic/priestly associations) rather than a secular term. PNEUMA's "government workers" eliminates the theological claim Paul is making: that governing authorities, in their proper function, serve a priestly role.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Ministers of God" retained for its theological resonance.

---

## 13:7 — "respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed" vs. "fear/honor"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the [one due] the fear, the fear; to the [one due] the honor, the honor" | "Fear" for φόβον — literal |
| LOGOS | "respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed" | "Respect" for φόβον |
| PNEUMA | "give respect and honor to those who are in authority" | Collapses the two into a single clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The key divergence is φόβον: EMET's "fear" is literal but carries connotations of terror in modern English. LOGOS's "respect" captures the social meaning of φόβος in this context (deference, not dread). PNEUMA collapses the parallel structure, losing Paul's rhetorical pattern.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Respect" for φόβον in this social context.

---

## 13:8 — "Owe no one anything, except to love each other"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Owe no one anything, except to love one another; for the [one] loving the other has fulfilled [the] law." | "One another" — reciprocal |
| LOGOS | "Owe no one anything, except to love each other, for the one who loves another has fulfilled the law." | "Each other" — slightly different reciprocal |
| PNEUMA | "Owe nothing to anyone — except for your obligation to love one another." | "Obligation to love" — adds "obligation"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three are close. Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "obligation" which, while interpreting the metaphor of permanent debt correctly, makes explicit what Paul leaves as implication.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:11 — "the hour has come for you to wake from sleep" vs. "time is running out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] hour already [has come] for you to be raised from sleep; for now the salvation [is] nearer to us than when we believed." | "Be raised from sleep" — passive; "the salvation" with article |
| LOGOS | "the hour has come for you to wake from sleep. For salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed." | "Wake from sleep" — active; "first believed" added |
| PNEUMA | "time is running out. Wake up, for our salvation is nearer now than when we first believed." | "Time is running out" — paraphrase; "Wake up" as separate imperative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "time is running out" replaces Paul's sleep/wake metaphor with a modern idiom that loses the resurrection overtones of the language. EMET's passive "be raised" is interesting but the Greek ἐγερθῆναι can be rendered either way.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:12 — "armor of light" vs. "weapons of the light" vs. "shining armor of right living"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let us put on the weapons of the light" | "Weapons" for ὅπλα — more literal (ὅπλα = weapons/instruments) |
| LOGOS | "put on the armor of light" | "Armor" — traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "put on the shining armor of right living" | "Shining" and "of right living" — interpretive additions |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "armor of light." EMET's "weapons" is technically correct (ὅπλα can mean weapons or armor), but "armor" better captures the protective/defensive connotation in this context (you "put on" armor, not weapons). PNEUMA adds "shining" and "of right living" — neither is in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:13 — "orgies" vs. "revels" vs. "wild parties"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not in revels and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and licentiousness, not in strife and jealousy" | "Revels" for κώμοις; "licentiousness" for ἀσελγείαις |
| LOGOS | "not in orgies and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and sensuality, not in quarreling and jealousy" | "Orgies" for κώμοις; "sensuality" for ἀσελγείαις |
| PNEUMA | "Don't participate in the darkness of wild parties and drunkenness, or in sexual promiscuity and immoral living, or in quarreling and jealousy." | "Wild parties"; adds "the darkness of"; "immoral living" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "revels" is slightly archaic. LOGOS's "orgies" captures the debauchery implied by κώμοι (originally nocturnal festivals). PNEUMA adds "the darkness of" (not in text) and "immoral living" is too general for ἀσέλγεια.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:14 — "put on the Lord Jesus Christ" vs. "clothe yourself with the presence of the Lord Jesus Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But put on the Lord Yeshua Christos, and do not make provision for the flesh, for [its] desires." | "Put on" literal; "Yeshua Christos" |
| LOGOS | "But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires." | Same structure; anglicized names |
| PNEUMA | "Instead, clothe yourself with the presence of the Lord Jesus Christ. And don't let yourself think about ways to indulge your evil desires." | "Clothe yourself with the presence" — adds "presence"; "evil desires" adds "evil" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "the presence of" to the "put on" metaphor, which changes the meaning from identity-formation (putting on Christ like a garment, cf. Galatians 3:27) to experiential awareness (being in Christ's presence). The baptismal clothing metaphor should be preserved without addition. PNEUMA also adds "evil" before "desires."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The baptismal "put on Christ" metaphor preserved.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA's secularization of theological vocabulary | vv.4, 6, 7 | "Power to punish" for "bear the sword"; "government workers" for "ministers of God" |
| PNEUMA's interpretive additions | vv.1, 4, 12, 13, 14 | "Must submit"; "sent"; "shining"; "the darkness of"; "the presence of" |
| Theological precision in key terms | vv.2, 5, 6, 7 | κρίμα, ὀργή, λειτουργοί, φόβος |
| EMET's archaic vocabulary | vv.7, 12, 13 | "Fear" (for respect), "revels," "weapons" |
| Register differences | vv.1, 11, 13 | EMET formal/archaic; LOGOS formal/readable; PNEUMA conversational |
| Metaphor preservation vs. interpretation | vv.11, 12, 14 | Sleep/wake; armor of light; put on Christ |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 13 is Paul's most politically sensitive chapter, and the translation divergences reflect different approaches to its sharp edges. The most consequential disagreements concern theological vocabulary: PNEUMA consistently secularizes Paul's deliberately theological language about the state ("ministers of God" becomes "government workers," "bear the sword" becomes "power to punish," "wrath" becomes "punishment"). This domesticates what Paul is doing — he is making a theological claim about the state's role in God's purposes, not merely describing government function. EMET preserves the full theological weight but in archaic English. LOGOS strikes the balance SOPHIA favors: retaining the theological terms in readable modern English. The chapter also shows the pattern of PNEUMA adding interpretive glosses to metaphors ("the presence of" Christ, "shining" armor, "evil" desires) that alter the meaning of compact Pauline expressions.

---
